structuredFacts.title: Napoleon Bonaparte
structuredFacts.subtitle: French general and emperor (1769–1821)
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Napoleon Bonaparte (born Napoleone di Buonaparte; 15 August 1769 – 5 May 1821), later known by his regnal name Napoleon I, was Emperor of the French from 18 May 1804 until his first abdication in 1814, with a brief restoration during the Hundred Days in 1815. He rose to prominence as a general during the French Revolution and led a series of military campaigns across Europe and the Middle East during the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars. As a statesman, he implemented numerous legal and administrative reforms in France and Europe.
Born on the island of Corsica to a family of Italian origin, Napoleon moved to mainland France in 1779 and was commissioned as an officer in the French Royal Army in 1785. He supported the French Revolution in 1789 and promoted its cause in Corsica. He rose rapidly through the ranks after winning the siege of Toulon in 1793 and defeating royalist insurgents in Paris on 13 Vendémiaire in 1795. In 1796 he commanded a military campaign against the Austrians and their Italian allies in the War of the First Coalition, scoring decisive victories and becoming a national hero. He led an invasion of Egypt and Syria in 1798, which served as a springboard for his rise to political power. In November 1799 Napoleon engineered the Coup of 18 Brumaire against the French Directory and became First Consul of the Republic. He won the Battle of Marengo in 1800, which secured France's victory in the War of the Second Coalition, and in 1803, he sold the territory of Louisiana to the United States. In December 1804 Napoleon crowned himself Emperor of the French, further consolidating his power.
The breakdown of the Treaty of Amiens led to the War of the Third Coalition by 1805. Napoleon shattered the coalition with a decisive victory at the Battle of Austerlitz, which led to the dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire. In the War of the Fourth Coalition, Napoleon defeated Prussia at the Battle of Jena–Auerstedt in 1806, marched his Grande Armée into Eastern Europe, and defeated the Russians in 1807 at the Battle of Friedland. Seeking to extend his trade embargo against Britain, Napoleon invaded the Iberian Peninsula and installed his brother Joseph as King of Spain in 1808, provoking the Peninsular War. In 1809 the Austrians again challenged France in the War of the Fifth Coalition, during which Napoleon consolidated his dominance over Europe after winning the Battle of Wagram. In the summer of 1812 he launched an invasion of Russia. After forcing the Russians to abandon Moscow following the Battle of Borodino, Napoleon briefly occupied the city before conducting a catastrophic retreat of his army that winter. In 1813 Prussia and Austria joined Russia in the War of the Sixth Coalition, in which Napoleon was decisively defeated at the Battle of Leipzig. The coalition invaded France and captured Paris, forcing Napoleon to abdicate in April 1814. He was exiled to the Mediterranean island of Elba and the House of Bourbons was restored to power. Ten months later Napoleon escaped from Elba with around a thousand men and marched on Paris, reclaiming control of the country. His opponents responded by forming a Seventh Coalition, which defeated him at the Battle of Waterloo in June 1815. Napoleon was exiled to the remote island of Saint Helena in the South Atlantic, where he died of stomach cancer in 1821, aged 51.
Napoleon is considered one of the greatest military commanders in history, and Napoleonic tactics are still studied at military schools worldwide. His legacy endures through the legal and administrative reforms he enacted in France and Western Europe, most notably the Napoleonic Code. He established a system of public education, abolished the vestiges of feudalism, emancipated Jews and other religious minorities, abolished the Spanish Inquisition, enacted the principle of equality before the law for an emerging middle class, and centralized state power at the expense of religious authorities. His conquests also acted as a catalyst for political change and the development of nation-states. However, he remains controversial because of his role in wars that devastated Europe, his looting of conquered territories, and his mixed record on civil rights. He abolished the free press, ended directly elected representative government, exiled and imprisoned critics of his regime, reinstated slavery in French colonies, banned the entry of black people and mulattos into France, reduced the civil rights of women and children, reintroduced a hereditary monarchy and nobility, and violently repressed popular uprisings against his rule.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Born in Ajaccio, Corsica, to parents of Italian noble descent, Napoleon Bonaparte faced a difficult youth. Classmates at Brienne-le-Château routinely bullied him for his short stature, Corsican accent, and poor French spelling. Later, severe financial strain forced him to condense his two-year course at Paris's École militaire into just twelve months, yet he still graduated as the first Corsican to finish at the prestigious academy. Upon graduation, Napoleon returned to his home island to begin his military career. By 1799, he engineered the Coup of 18 Brumaire against the French Directory to become First Consul, ultimately consolidating absolute rule by crowning himself Emperor of the French in 1804.

cards.title: The Making of Bonaparte
cards.summary: Born in Ajaccio, Corsica, in 1769, Napoleon Bonaparte was the son of noble Italian parents. To secure their social advancement after France annexed the island, his family sent him to French military academies. At Brienne-le-Château, classmates routinely bullied the young outsider for his short stature, thick accent, and poor spelling. This harsh isolation only drove him to complete his elite education rapidly and secure an artillery commission. By finishing the course in just one year, he graduated as the first Corsican from the prestigious École militaire in Paris. Ultimately, this relentless resilience through early adversity forged France's most formidable military commander.
cards.readerQuestion: How did a boy from a Mediterranean island become a French military officer?
cards.keyTakeaway: Adversity and elite training forged France's greatest military commander.

cards.title: The Rise of Napoleon Bonaparte
cards.summary: Napoleon Bonaparte returned to Corsica in 1786, eager to launch his ambitious military career. Amid the chaotic instability of revolutionary France, this determined outsider capitalized on his elite training and tactical brilliance. Years earlier, the legendary scientist Pierre-Simon Laplace had personally examined the young cadet during his final academy trials. Building on these early triumphs, Napoleon eventually overthrew the Directory in a swift coup to become the absolute ruler of France. Yet, despite wielding supreme power, the emperor spoke French with a heavy, native Corsican accent throughout his entire life. In the end, an accented outsider had weaponized sheer military genius to seize total control of the French Republic.
cards.readerQuestion: How did an outsider rise through the ranks to seize absolute political power?
cards.keyTakeaway: An accented outsider weaponized military genius to seize French rule.

cards.title: The Price of Empire
cards.summary: In 1804, Napoleon Bonaparte seized absolute control of France and crowned himself Emperor. To wage his planned military campaigns across Europe, he urgently required both absolute domestic authority and financial capital. He thus sold the massive territory of Louisiana to the United States in 1803. By liquidating these vast North American lands, the French ruler successfully secured the wealth needed to finance his grand army. This strategic transaction was executed specifically to fund his global conquests. Ultimately, Napoleon financed his imperial expansion by transferring Louisiana to American hands.
cards.readerQuestion: How did Napoleon consolidate his rule and fund his global military ambitions?
cards.keyTakeaway: Napoleon funded his imperial expansion by selling Louisiana to America.

cards.title: The Fall and Return
cards.summary: In 1812, Napoleon launched a massive invasion of Russia and briefly occupied Moscow, only to suffer a catastrophic winter retreat. This disastrous campaign decimated his military strength, leaving him highly vulnerable to the Sixth Coalition. Forced to abdicate his throne in 1814, the defeated emperor was exiled to the island of Elba. He soon escaped this first exile with only about a thousand men, successfully marching on Paris to reclaim his empire. Yet following his final defeat at Waterloo, Napoleon was permanently banished to the remote island of Saint Helena. Ultimately, it was that disastrous Russian campaign that had set his final downfall in motion.
cards.readerQuestion: What led to the collapse of his empire and his final defeat?
cards.keyTakeaway: A disastrous Russian campaign led to Napoleon's final downfall.

cards.title: The Contradictory Imperial Shadow
cards.summary: To centralize French state power and establish civil reforms, Napoleon introduced the Napoleonic Code. He sought to consolidate absolute power and stabilize his empire through these centralized legal measures alongside colonial economic control. Across Western Europe, he abolished the Spanish Inquisition and emancipated religious minorities. Yet, his actions created a deeply polarized legacy that paired progressive European legal reforms with brutal colonial subjugation and systemic racism. Specifically, he reinstated slavery in French colonies and banned black and mulatto people from entering France. Ultimately, Napoleon's progressive reforms coexisted with brutal, authoritarian oppression.
cards.readerQuestion: Why does Napoleon's legal and social legacy remain deeply polarized?
cards.keyTakeaway: Napoleon's progressive reforms coexisted with brutal, authoritarian oppression.
